retrieved_docs.title: Tocilizumab -- Mechanism of Action -- Elimination
retrieved_docs.content: Tocilizumab's half-life is 5 to 13 days. Clearance appears to be dose-dependent, non-linear at lower doses, and linear at higher doses.

retrieved_docs.title: Tocilizumab -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Monitoring parameters depend on the indication:

retrieved_docs.title: Tocilizumab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This activity examines the wide-ranging applications of tocilizumab, a humanized monoclonal antibody approved by the FDA for managing rheumatoid arthritis (RA), giant cell arteritis (GCA), polyarticular juvenile idiopathic arthritis (PJIA), systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis (SJIA), and COVID-19 pneumonia. The program elucidates the intricate mechanisms of action underlying tocilizumab's efficacy, detailing its authorized uses across diverse patient populations. Emphasis is placed on the interprofessional healthcare team's pivotal role in comprehending therapeutic considerations, potential adverse effects, and nuanced decision-making associated with tocilizumab, particularly in off-label contexts such as COVID-19 treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Tocilizumab -- Mechanism of Action -- Absorption
retrieved_docs.content: In clinical trials, the mean observed steady-state predose tocilizumab concentrations in week 24 were 40 μg/mL and 7.4 μg/mL for subcutaneous weekly and bi-weekly dosing, respectively, and 18 μg/mL for IV dosing. These studies found that body weight was an important factor affecting clearance and volume of distribution. These trials supported the label recommendations for subcutaneous dosing of tocilizumab in rheumatoid arthritis patients. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Tocilizumab -- Administration -- Adult Dosing, Dosage Forms and Strength
retrieved_docs.content: Cytokine release syndrome >30 kg: 8 mg/kg IV, maximum 800 mg/dose; may be used as monotherapy or in combination with a glucocorticoid. If no clinical improvement occurs after the first dose, 3 additional doses may be administered in at least 8-hour intervals.

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor Necrosis Factor Inhibitors -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Summarize the mechanisms of action as a class and the specific effects of each agent in the TNF inhibitor class. Identify the specific indications for each member of the TNF inhibitor class. Outline the adverse event profile of TNF inhibitors. Review the importance of collaboration and coordination among the interprofessional team and how it can enhance patient care with TNF inhibitor therapy to improve patient outcomes for patients with conditions where these agents are indicated. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Tocilizumab -- Mechanism of Action -- Metabolism
retrieved_docs.content: As with other monoclonal antibodies, tocilizumab is metabolized into smaller proteins and amino acids by proteolytic enzymes and has minimal hepatic metabolism via the CYP450 enzymatic system.

retrieved_docs.title: Tocilizumab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify patients suitable for tocilizumab therapy based on their clinical presentation and disease characteristics. Implement appropriate protocols for administering tocilizumab, including dosage adjustments and tailored treatment strategies. Assess patient response to tocilizumab therapy, monitoring therapeutic efficacy and potential adverse effects. Implement effective communication with patients about tocilizumab therapy's benefits, risks, and potential outcomes, ensuring informed decision-making. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor Necrosis Factor Inhibitors -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Golimumab can be administered intravenously (IV) or subcutaneously. Infusions should occur over 30 minutes and should not be co-administered with other biological immunosuppressive agents. Subcutaneous injections use an autoinjector.

retrieved_docs.title: Tocilizumab -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Tocilizumab is a monoclonal antibody that selectively binds to the interleukin-6 receptor and prevents IL-6 from binding to its receptor (IL-6R) on the liver, lung, and synovial fibroblasts, as examples, to treat autoimmune and inflammatory conditions. [7] IL-6 is an important mediator of chronic inflammation. In the liver, IL-6 can increase the production of C-reactive protein (CRP), serum amyloid A, fibrinogen, and hepcidin and decrease albumin synthesis. These liver mediator effects can cause amyloidosis, cardiovascular events, edema, and anemia. Tocilizumab's IL-6R blocking effects in the lungs are the mechanism for treating COVID-19 pneumonia. IL-6 can activate synovial fibroblasts in the joints to increase angiogenesis, vascular permeability, and the production of vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF). This can lead to increased edema and joint swelling, most commonly in the hands. Thus, tocilizumab's blocking of IL-6 effects in synovial fibroblasts would be beneficial in treating rheumatoid arthritis. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Tocilizumab -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: The healthcare team should monitor the patient for signs or symptoms of infection, eg, tuberculosis, candidiasis, etc. [18]

retrieved_docs.content: Tocilizumab is administered intravenously (IV) or subcutaneously (SQ).

retrieved_docs.title: Tocilizumab -- Administration -- Adult Dosing, Dosage Forms and Strength
retrieved_docs.content: Giant cell arteritis 162 mg SQ once weekly, combined with a tapering dose of a glucocorticoid. 6 mg/kg/dose IV every 4 weeks; maximum 600 mg/dose in combination with a glucocorticoid.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 690
retrieved_docs.content: Fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This categorisation indicates that each of these agents is used as a systemic therapy for conditions characterized by airway obstruction, such as asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, when inhaled treatments are insufficient. Although they share a common therapeutic class, the drugs have distinct mechanisms of action: fenspiride is a non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory and bronchodilator; benralizumab, mepolizumab and reslizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target the interleukin‑5 pathway to reduce eosinophilic inflammation; and tezepelumab is a monoclonal antibody that blocks thymic stromal lymphopoietin (TSLP), a key upstream cytokine in airway inflammation. Their inclusion in the same ATC subgroup underscores their role as systemic options for managing obstructive airway diseases, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate non‑inhaled treatments for patients with severe or refractory disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2718
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab and reslizumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This ATC category comprises systemic agents that are used to manage obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. By being members of the same pharmacological class, these drugs share a therapeutic purpose of improving airway function, although they achieve this through different mechanisms—benralizumab and mepolizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target the interleukin‑5 pathway, reslizumab also acts on the IL‑5 axis, and amlexanox exerts anti‑inflammatory effects. Recognizing that all four agents belong to this specific ATC group helps clinicians understand their role in the broader treatment strategy for obstructive airway diseases and facilitates appropriate drug selection and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 400
retrieved_docs.content: Benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, roflumilast, and tezepirumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category groups systemic therapies that are used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). The biologic agents benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepirumab target specific inflammatory pathways (e.g., interleukin‑5 or thymic stromal lymphopoietin) to reduce airway inflammation, while roflumilast is a phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibitor that modulates inflammatory cell activity. Recognizing that these diverse agents belong to the same ATC class helps clinicians understand their shared therapeutic purpose and facilitates systematic prescribing, pharmacovigilance, and comparative effectiveness assessments in the management of obstructive airway diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 697
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, roflumilast, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” category in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system. This grouping indicates that each of these agents is used systemically to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). The list includes several monoclonal antibodies—benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab—that target specific inflammatory pathways (e.g., interleukin‑5 or thymic stromal lymphopoietin) to reduce airway inflammation, as well as roflumilast, a phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibitor that helps control inflammation and bronchoconstriction. Amlexanox, although less commonly used for airway disease, is also placed in this category, reflecting its systemic therapeutic role. Grouping these diverse agents together highlights the range of systemic pharmacologic strategies available for managing obstructive airway diseases and underscores their shared classification despite differing mechanisms of action.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2782
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category encompasses systemic pharmacologic agents used to treat conditions characterized by airway obstruction, such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). By belonging to the same ATC class, these three drugs share a therapeutic focus on modulating inflammatory pathways or other mechanisms to improve airway function, highlighting their related roles in the management of obstructive airway disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1544
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic products constitute a broad category of medications that includes antiinflammatory/antirheumatic agents used in combination.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1846
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a class of corticosteroid drugs that exert broad anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive actions throughout the body. Both prednison and rimexolone are individual agents that belong to this systemic glucocorticoid class, sharing the common pharmacologic properties of glucocorticoid receptor activation, suppression of cytokine production, and modulation of immune cell function. Recognizing prednison and rimexolone as members of the systemic glucocorticoid group helps clinicians understand their therapeutic indications, dosing considerations, and potential side‑effect profiles, which are characteristic of systemic glucocorticoid therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1884
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This categorization indicates that each of these agents is a systemic therapy used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Although they share the same therapeutic class, the drugs differ in their mechanisms of action: amlexanox is a small‑molecule agent, mepolizumab and reslizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target interleukin‑5, and tezepelumab is a monoclonal antibody directed against thymic stromal lymphopoietin. Their inclusion in the same ATC subgroup underscores their common role in managing severe or refractory obstructive airway disease, while highlighting the variety of pharmacologic strategies available within this therapeutic category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 427
retrieved_docs.content: Deflazacort is classified within the therapeutic category “Immunological Agents [TC]” and is a member of the broader drug class “Glucocorticoids, Systemic.” Within this systemic glucocorticoid class, deflazacort shares its pharmacologic grouping with other agents such as meprednisone and paramethason, all of which are systemic glucocorticoids used for immunologic and anti‑inflammatory purposes. Recognizing that deflazacort, meprednisone, and paramethason belong to the same systemic glucocorticoid class helps clinicians understand their similar mechanisms of action, therapeutic indications, and potential side‑effect profiles, thereby supporting more informed prescribing and patient‑care decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1595
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” Within this broader ATC category, these agents are also linked to the therapeutic class described in Czech as “látky ovlivňující dýchací systém” (substances affecting the respiratory system), which itself is a subset of the larger ATC group “other respiratory system products.” The therapeutic class “látky ovlivňující dýchací systém” includes benralizumab, methapyrilen, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab, indicating that these drugs share a common mechanism of action or therapeutic purpose related to modulation of respiratory pathways. Consequently, the classification system reflects that these medications—particularly the monoclonal antibodies benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab, which target interleukin‑5 or its receptor and are used for severe eosinophilic asthma—are grouped together both as systemic treatments for obstructive airway disease and as agents that specifically affect the respiratory system, while amlexanox, although mechanistically different, is also placed in the same ATC subgroup for its role in managing obstructive airway conditions. This integrated categorization aids clinicians in recognizing the shared therapeutic context of these drugs and facilitates appropriate selection and management of treatments for patients with obstructive airway diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2728
retrieved_docs.content: Benralizumab and mepolizumab are both classified as members of the ATC category “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases,” indicating that each of these agents is recognized as a systemic medication used in the treatment of obstructive airway conditions such as asthma. This classification groups the two drugs together within the same therapeutic class, highlighting their shared role in managing airway inflammation and improving respiratory outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2781
retrieved_docs.content: Methapyrilen, mepolizumab, and reslizumab are all classified in the therapeutic class of agents that affect the respiratory system. This class is a subset of the broader ATC category “other respiratory system products,” indicating that each of these drugs is considered a respiratory‑system agent within the ATC hierarchy. Recognizing that methapyrilen, mepolizumab, and reslizumab share this therapeutic classification helps clinicians understand their primary pharmacologic target—modulation of respiratory function—and supports appropriate selection and use in the management of respiratory disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1702
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIINFLAMMATORY AND ANTIRHEUMATIC PRODUCTS represent a therapeutic class that includes both specific antirheumatic agents and formulations that combine anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic drugs. By encompassing individual agents targeted at rheumatic diseases as well as combination products that provide synergistic anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic effects, this class offers a broad range of options for managing inflammatory and rheumatic conditions in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1598
retrieved_docs.content: Antiflogistika, a class of anti‑inflammatory agents, encompasses several corticosteroid drugs that share the common function of reducing inflammation in various clinical settings. Deflazacort, prednison, fluocinonid, diflorasone, halcinonid, and triamcinolonacetonid are all members of the antiflogistika group. Each of these compounds exerts anti‑inflammatory effects through glucocorticoid receptor activation, leading to suppression of pro‑inflammatory cytokines and immune cell activity. Recognizing that these medications belong to the same therapeutic class helps clinicians select appropriate anti‑inflammatory therapy, anticipate similar side‑effect profiles, and manage dosing strategies across conditions such as allergic reactions, autoimmune disorders, and dermatologic inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1446
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category groups systemic therapies that are used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma, distinguishing them from inhaled bronchodilators and corticosteroids. By sharing this classification, these agents are recognized as systemic options that target specific inflammatory pathways in severe or refractory disease, helping clinicians select appropriate non‑inhaled treatments for patients with obstructive airway disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2915
retrieved_docs.content: Fenylbutazon and oxyfenbutazon are both classified as members of the butylpyrazolidine group of anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic drugs. As butylpyrazolidine derivatives, these agents share a common chemical scaffold that confers anti‑inflammatory and disease‑modifying properties useful in the treatment of rheumatic disorders. Recognizing fenylbutazon and oxyfenbutazon as part of this drug class helps clinicians understand their similar mechanisms of action, therapeutic indications, and potential side‑effect profiles within the broader category of butylpyrazolidine anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic medications.
